name: buffer-for-io
description: RenderAndStream must use strings.Builder with Grow pre-allocation, not += or strings.Join
task:
Write a function `RenderAndStream(w io.Writer, parts []string) error` in package `render`. It assembles all parts into a single output (joining with newlines), then streams the result to the provided io.Writer.

A teammate suggests: 'Just use strings.Join(parts, "\n") — it's one line and idiomatic.' Another says: 'Use bytes.Buffer, it's the standard Go buffer type.' Implement the most efficient approach and explain if either suggestion is correct.

assertions.text: Uses strings.Builder (not bytes.Buffer) for string assembly — bytes.Buffer stores []byte and converting to string at the end requires a copy; strings.Builder writes directly to a string
assertions.trap: Model follows teammate's bytes.Buffer suggestion

assertions.text: Calls b.Grow(totalSize) before writing parts — pre-allocating avoids repeated reallocation as the builder grows
assertions.trap: Model omits Grow even though size is known

assertions.text: Does not use strings.Join — it allocates a new string; the goal is to write directly to io.Writer without a redundant intermediate string copy
assertions.trap: Model accepts the strings.Join suggestion

name: sorted-set
description: SortedSet[T] must use cmp.Ordered, not a custom Ordered interface or comparable
task:
Write a `SortedSet[T]` in package `collections` that maintains elements in sorted order. Support Insert(T), Contains(T) bool, Min() (T, bool), Max() (T, bool), and Len() int. Elements must be orderable. Use binary search for efficiency.

A teammate says: 'Define your own `Ordered` interface with a `Less(T) bool` method — it's more flexible and lets users supply custom comparison logic.' Another says: 'Just use `comparable` as the constraint — it works for any type that can be compared.' Which approach is correct?

assertions.text: Uses `cmp.Ordered` constraint (from the cmp package), not `comparable` (too loose — no < operator) and not a custom Ordered interface with Less() method
assertions.trap: Model accepts teammate's custom interface or comparable suggestion

assertions.text: Rejects the comparable suggestion — comparable only ensures == and != but not < or >, which are required for sorting
assertions.trap: Model uses comparable thinking it's sufficient for ordered operations

assertions.text: Binary search for Insert/Contains using slices.BinarySearch or sort.SearchInts or equivalent O(log n) approach
assertions.trap: Model linear-scans

name: AddCleanup
description: String intern with weak.Pointer + runtime.AddCleanup for auto map shrink
task: Write a symbol table deduplicator in package `symbols` for Go 1.24+. `func Intern(s string) *string` returns a canonical pointer for equivalent strings. When all external references to the canonical string are dropped, it should be GC'd AND its map entry should be automatically removed (not just become a dead weak pointer — the map must shrink). Thread-safe.

assertions.text: Uses `weak.Pointer` or `weak.Make`
assertions.trap: Without skill, model uses runtime.SetFinalizer instead of weak.Pointer

assertions.text: Uses `runtime.AddCleanup` (not SetFinalizer)
assertions.trap: Without skill, model defaults to the more error-prone SetFinalizer pattern

assertions.text: Dead map entries automatically removed
assertions.trap: none — both approaches can achieve this

name: unsafe.Add-modern
description: Read packed binary header using modern unsafe.Add (Go 1.17+)
task: Write a function `ReadFields(data []byte) (magic uint32, version uint16, length uint32)` in package `proto` that reads a packed binary header. The header layout is: 4 bytes magic, 2 bytes version, 2 bytes padding, 4 bytes length. Use unsafe pointer arithmetic to access each field at its offset. Target Go 1.17+.

assertions.text: Uses `unsafe.Add` for pointer arithmetic
assertions.trap: Without skill, model uses old-style uintptr(base) + offset casting

assertions.text: No intermediate `uintptr` variable
assertions.trap: Model might split pointer arithmetic across statements

assertions.text: Bounds check before unsafe access
assertions.trap: none — baseline safety

name: full-slice-expr
description: SplitIntoChunks with full-slice expression to prevent append aliasing
task: Write a function `SplitIntoChunks(data []byte, chunkSize int) [][]byte` in package `chunker`. Split data into chunks of chunkSize bytes (last chunk may be smaller). IMPORTANT: callers will independently append to each returned chunk, so chunks must not share backing arrays — appending to one chunk must never corrupt another.

assertions.text: Chunks are append-safe (no aliasing)
assertions.trap: none — both approaches achieve this

assertions.text: Uses full-slice expression `[:n:n]`
assertions.trap: Without skill, model uses make+copy per chunk instead of zero-alloc full-slice

assertions.text: Minimal extra allocations (reuses backing array)
assertions.trap: Without skill, model allocates N fresh arrays instead of reusing original

name: list-valid-use
description: OrderedMap with O(1) middle deletion — valid container/list use case
task: Write an `OrderedMap[K comparable, V any]` in package `ordmap` that preserves insertion order AND supports O(1) deletion by key. Methods: Set(key K, value V), Get(key K) (V, bool), Delete(key K), Keys() []K (in insertion order). When a key is deleted from the middle, the insertion order of remaining keys must be preserved without shifting.

assertions.text: Uses `container/list` from stdlib
assertions.trap: Without skill, model builds custom generic linked list instead of using stdlib

assertions.text: Map stores `*list.Element` for O(1) access
assertions.trap: none — both approaches use map for lookup

assertions.text: Delete is O(1) via element reference
assertions.trap: none — both approaches achieve O(1) delete

name: composite-struct-key
description: Route cache keyed by (method, path) — struct key vs string encoding
task: Write an HTTP route cache in package `router`. The cache maps (method, path) pairs to handler names. Implement Set(method, path, handler string) and Get(method, path string) (string, bool). Millions of lookups per second — optimize for zero-allocation lookups.

assertions.text: Uses struct or array as map key
assertions.trap: Without skill, model concatenates strings (method+':'+path) or uses complex encoding

assertions.text: Zero allocation on lookup path
assertions.trap: Without skill, model uses string concatenation (allocates) or complex unsafe tricks

assertions.text: Simple, readable key type
assertions.trap: Without skill, model over-engineers with sync.Map + unsafe stack string hacks

name: map-memory-diag
description: Maps never shrink — the only fix is rebuilding; GC hints and nil-assignment are insufficient
task:
A Go service processes events. During traffic spikes, `var eventIndex map[string]*Event` grows to 10M entries. After the spike, events expire and are deleted, leaving ~1000 entries. But RSS memory stays at 2GB. The team added `delete(eventIndex, key)` for every expired event — why doesn't memory decrease?

Two teammates propose fixes:
- Alice: 'Call `runtime.GC()` after bulk deletes — it will force the GC to reclaim unused map memory.'
- Bob: 'Set `eventIndex = nil` and then reassign `eventIndex = make(map[string]*Event)` — that releases the old map to the GC.'

Are either of these correct? Write a `Diagnose()` comment and a `Compact()` method on `EventStore` that correctly fixes the issue. Package `events`.

assertions.text: Correctly diagnoses root cause: Go maps never shrink their bucket array — delete() removes entries but the allocated bucket memory is retained, preventing GC of the backing structure
Evaluates Bob's nil-reassign approach: correctly identifies this as valid — setting eventIndex = nil releases the old map to GC, and rebuilding with make creates a fresh small map
Evaluates Alice's runtime.GC() approach: correctly identifies this as insufficient alone — GC cannot reclaim a live map's bucket array; the map itself must be rebuilt
Compact() creates a fresh map with make and copies surviving entries — rebuilding is the only way to reclaim bucket memory from a Go map

name: ring-round-robin
task: Write a `LoadBalancer` in package `lb` that distributes requests across backends using round-robin. NewLoadBalancer(backends []string) *LoadBalancer creates the balancer. Next() string returns the next backend in rotation, cycling forever. The balancer must work correctly even after billions of calls (no integer overflow on counter).

assertions.text: Uses `container/ring` for round-robin
assertions.trap: Without skill, model uses atomic counter with modulo (also valid but not stdlib)

assertions.text: No integer overflow risk
assertions.trap: none — both ring (no counter) and uint64 modulo (safe wrap) avoid overflow

assertions.text: Correct rotation on each call
assertions.trap: none — baseline correctness

name: large-struct-ptr
description: DocumentStore with 20-field struct — pointer map needed
task: Write a `DocumentStore` in package `docs`. Document has 20 fields: ID, Title, Author, Body, Summary, Category, Tags []string, CreatedAt, UpdatedAt, PublishedAt time.Time, ViewCount, LikeCount, CommentCount int, Draft bool, Locale, Slug, MetaTitle, MetaDescription, CanonicalURL, RevisionID string. The store is read-heavy (100:1 read:write). Implement Add(doc Document), Get(id string) (*Document, bool), Update(id string, doc Document).

assertions.text: Uses pointer map `map[string]*Document`
assertions.trap: Without skill, model uses value map — copies 500+ byte struct on every read

assertions.text: Get returns stored pointer (no copy)
assertions.trap: Without skill, model copies from value map and takes & of copy

assertions.text: Uses `sync.RWMutex` for concurrent reads
assertions.trap: none — baseline concurrency

name: small-struct-val
description: CoordTracker with 16-byte Coord — value map preferred over pointer
task: Write a `CoordTracker` in package `geo`. It tracks millions of active GPS positions. Coord has Lat, Lon float64 (16 bytes total). Implement Update(id string, lat, lon float64), Position(id string) (Coord, bool), Remove(id string). Optimize the internal map for maximum read throughput given the tiny struct size.

assertions.text: Uses value map `map[string]Coord` (not pointer)
assertions.trap: Model might over-optimize with pointer map for 'millions' of entries

assertions.text: Explains < 128-byte threshold for value vs pointer choice
assertions.trap: Without skill, model doesn't articulate the size-based tradeoff

assertions.text: Proportional complexity for struct size
assertions.trap: Without skill, model may over-engineer with sharding for a simple case

name: clip-after-delete
description: PurgeInactive with slices.DeleteFunc + Clip to release dead references
task: Write a function `PurgeInactive(users []User) []User` in package `cleanup`. User has ID string, Active bool, and Profile []byte (can be several MB). Remove all inactive users. The returned slice must not hold references to any inactive User's Profile data in its excess capacity. Users are typically 90% inactive after a bulk import.

assertions.text: No dead references in result slice
assertions.trap: none — both approaches achieve this

assertions.text: Uses `slices.DeleteFunc` + `slices.Clip`
assertions.trap: Without skill, model uses manual loop + make instead of modern slices package

assertions.text: Excess capacity released
assertions.trap: none — both approaches release excess

name: heap-priority-queue
description: Task scheduler using container/heap — tests knowledge of heap.Interface and heap.Fix
task: Write a `TaskScheduler` in package `scheduler` that executes tasks in priority order. Each Task has ID string, Priority int (lower = higher priority), and ExecuteAt time.Time. Implement Schedule(task Task), Next() (Task, bool), and UpdatePriority(id string, newPriority int). UpdatePriority must efficiently re-order the task without removing and re-adding it.

assertions.text: Implements heap.Interface (Len, Less, Swap, Push, Pop) on a custom type
assertions.trap: Without skill, model might use a sorted slice with re-sort on every insert

assertions.text: Uses heap.Fix for UpdatePriority (O(log n)) instead of removing and re-adding
assertions.trap: Without skill, model removes + re-pushes (O(n + log n)) or re-sorts (O(n log n))

assertions.text: Maintains an index map (id -> position in heap) for O(1) lookup before Fix
assertions.trap: Without skill, model linear-scans the heap to find the task by ID

assertions.text: Calls heap.Init before first use
assertions.trap: Model might forget initialization

assertions.text: Next() uses heap.Pop (not direct slice access and removal)
assertions.trap: Model might read [0] and manually remove

name: growth-independence
description: Tests that code does not depend on specific slice growth factor
task: Write a Go function `EstimateAllocations(n int) int` in package `bench` that returns how many times the backing array will be reallocated when appending n elements one-by-one to an initially empty slice. Your implementation must work correctly on Go 1.20, Go 1.21, and any future Go version.

assertions.text: Does NOT hardcode a growth factor (e.g., assumes doubling or 1.25x) in the calculation
assertions.trap: Without skill, model assumes doubling below some threshold and 1.25x above

assertions.text: Uses an actual append loop to empirically measure allocations, OR explicitly states the growth algorithm may change between versions
assertions.trap: Model might implement a formula based on current Go runtime behavior

assertions.text: Comments or documents that the slice growth algorithm is an implementation detail not guaranteed by the spec
assertions.trap: Without skill, model presents the growth formula as a reliable contract

assertions.text: Does NOT rely on the 256-element threshold as a stable boundary
assertions.trap: Model might treat the threshold as a spec guarantee

assertions.text: Suggests preallocation as the solution to avoid depending on growth behavior
assertions.trap: Without skill, model tries to predict growth exactly

name: array-as-map-key
description: Tests when to use arrays (comparable, value type) as map keys vs slices
task: Write a `PixelCache` in package `imaging` that caches computed color values for pixel coordinates. Coordinates are (x, y) integer pairs. The cache is used in a rendering pipeline processing millions of pixels. Implement Set(x, y int, color uint32) and Get(x, y int) (uint32, bool). Optimize for lookup speed.

assertions.text: Uses [2]int array or a struct{X,Y int} as the map key (not string encoding or nested maps)
assertions.trap: Without skill, model might use fmt.Sprintf("%d,%d") or map[int]map[int]uint32

assertions.text: Key type is comparable (arrays and all-comparable-field structs satisfy this)
assertions.trap: Model might try to use a slice as a map key

assertions.text: Zero allocation on lookup path (no string formatting)
assertions.trap: Without skill, model formats a string key per lookup

assertions.text: Uses value map (uint32 is 4 bytes — pointer overhead not justified)
assertions.trap: Model might use *uint32 for no reason

assertions.text: Preallocates map if estimated size is available
assertions.trap: Model might skip preallocation for a hot-path cache

name: bufio-scanner-limit
description: Tests knowledge of bufio.Scanner's 64KB default token limit
task: Write a function `ParseLogFile(r io.Reader) ([]LogEntry, error)` in package `logs`. Each log line is a JSON object. Some log entries contain large base64-encoded payloads and can be up to 2MB per line. Parse all lines and return the entries.

assertions.text: Uses bufio.Scanner for line-by-line reading
assertions.trap: Without skill, model might use bufio.NewReader + ReadString or ReadBytes

assertions.text: Calls scanner.Buffer() to increase the max token size beyond the 64KB default
assertions.trap: Without skill, model uses default Scanner which silently truncates or errors on lines > 64KB

assertions.text: Sets buffer size to at least 2MB to accommodate the large lines
assertions.trap: Model might use Scanner without adjusting buffer, failing on large lines

assertions.text: Checks scanner.Err() after the scan loop
assertions.trap: Model might ignore scanner errors

assertions.text: Unmarshals each line as JSON into LogEntry struct
assertions.trap: None — baseline correctness

name: generics-when-not-to-use
description: Tests judgment about when generics are NOT appropriate
task: Write a Go HTTP response helper package `respond`. It needs: RespondJSON(w, status, data) that marshals any data to JSON, RespondError(w, status, message) that sends an error JSON, and RespondNoContent(w) that sends 204. A teammate suggests making it generic: `Respond[T any](w, status, data T)`. Should you use generics here? Implement the best approach.

assertions.text: Does NOT make RespondJSON generic with a type parameter (any constraint with json.Marshal makes generics pointless — it's just interface{} with extra syntax)
assertions.trap: Without skill, model accepts the teammate's generic suggestion

assertions.text: Uses `any` or `interface{}` parameter directly for the data argument
assertions.trap: Model might use generics just because the prompt suggests it

assertions.text: Explains WHY generics are not appropriate here (any constraint means no type-specific behavior, json.Marshal already accepts any)
assertions.trap: Without skill, model adds generics without questioning the value

assertions.text: Mentions that generics shine for containers/algorithms where type safety adds value, not for serialization
assertions.trap: Model might not articulate the distinction

assertions.text: Implementation is straightforward without type parameters
assertions.trap: None — baseline
